title: Western Hemisphere Nearshoring Act
plain_summary: This bill helps American companies move their factories and operations from China to countries in Latin America and the Caribbean. The US government will give these companies money, lower taxes, and other benefits to help them make this move. In return, goods made in these Latin American and Caribbean countries will have lower or no taxes when they come to America. The bill also lets the US government make new trade deals with these countries and sell them nuclear power equipment. The goal is to rely less on China for manufacturing and to create more jobs in nearby countries, which could help reduce illegal immigration to the US.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such prov

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Western Hemisphere Nearshoring Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress believes these facts are true. Our neighboring countries in North, Central, and South America are important for keeping peace, security, and democracy strong. When these nearby countries do not have stable governments or good job opportunities, it causes people to move to other countries in ways that break immigration laws. In two thousand twenty one, the United States sold one hundred seventy four billion six hundred twenty million dollars worth of goods to Central and South America. We bought one hundred twenty one billion dollars worth of goods from them. This means we sell more to them than we buy from them. When countries in our hemisphere grow their economies and develop, it makes the whole region stronger and more stable. There are many opportunities to increase trade of goods and services in our hemisphere. Making closer relationships with other American countries through free trade agreements would help economic growth and stability in the region. The United States should use its influence to encourage these countries to have private businesses, free markets, and work together on economic issues. Countries in the region should fight corruption, make their laws stronger, reduce unnecessary government paperwork, make it easier to get permits, and support free market ideas to encourage private companies to invest. With help from the United States, countries in the region must take serious steps to reduce illegal immigration. Countries in our hemisphere depend too much on China for their supply chains, which makes them vulnerable. Free trade and stronger business relationships between the United States and our hemisphere partners will help us work together on economics and business. This will create more investment opportunities, reduce illegal immigration, make us less dependent on China, and create jobs for American workers.

sections.title: Section 3  Use of United States International Development Finance Corporation Funds to Finance Moving Expenses and Necessary Workforce Development Costs Incurred by Companies Moving from the People's Republic of China to Latin America or the Caribbean
sections.text: The United States International Development Finance Corporation (a government agency that helps finance development projects) must work with other government agencies to help companies move from China to Latin America or the Caribbean. These other agencies include the United States Trade and Development Agency, the Export Import Bank, the Army Corps of Engineers, and the Agency for International Development. The DFC must also work with American representatives at international banks like the World Bank, the Inter American Development Bank, and the International Monetary Fund. Every year after this law starts, the DFC must use at least ten percent of its available money to help pay for qualified moving costs and worker training costs for companies that qualify for this help. The DFC must also give these companies lower interest rates on loans. The interest rate will be either the Federal funds rate (the basic interest rate set by the Federal Reserve) or the company's normal rate reduced by at least half a percent but no more than one percent. The rate cannot go below zero percent. Whichever rate is lower will be used. If the DFC does not use all the money set aside for this program in one year, it can use that leftover money the next year for this program or other DFC programs in Latin America or the Caribbean. The DFC cannot provide this help unless the Secretary of Commerce decides that helping the company will not hurt jobs in the United States. Companies that get this financing must return any money they do not use within a time period that the DFC decides for each case. The DFC can only give loans to companies if the loans make business sense and the DFC decides they can be paid back. The DFC will decide how much time companies have to pay back their loans. Within one hundred eighty days after this law starts, the DFC must create and send a plan to the House Foreign Affairs Committee and the Senate Foreign Relations Committee. This plan must show how to make it easier and faster to provide this help to companies.

sections.title: Section 4  Authority to Provide Duty Free Treatment for Goods and Services of Companies Moving from the People's Republic of China to Latin America or the Caribbean
sections.text: The President must announce that goods or services made in Latin American or Caribbean countries by qualified companies that received help under section three will not have to pay import taxes (called duties) or will get other special treatment when brought into the United States. The President can set whatever terms and conditions are needed for this special treatment. The President must create rules to make this section work. This duty free treatment will last for fifteen years starting from the date the company begins operating in the Latin American or Caribbean country. After the fifteen year period ends, nothing in this section stops goods and services from these companies from getting duty free treatment if goods from that country normally qualify for such treatment under other laws.

sections.title: Section 5  Additional Conditions on Receipt of Assistance Under Section 3 and Duty Free Treatment (or Other Preferential Treatment) Under Section 4
sections.text: Government agencies cannot provide help under section three or duty free treatment under section four to a company unless several conditions are met. First, the agency must decide that the company will create a reasonable number of jobs in the Latin American or Caribbean country it moves to, based on how much help the company gets. Second, the company must make a binding promise that after it gets help, it will never be owned or controlled by the government of China, the Chinese Communist Party, the government of Russia, or any other foreign enemy country. The company also promises it will not have its headquarters in China, Russia, or any other foreign enemy country. Third, within two years after getting help, and possibly with an extension if the agency thinks it is needed, the company must move all its business assets from China to a Latin American or Caribbean country. Fourth, the company must keep all its business assets in a Latin American or Caribbean country after the move is complete. The appropriate government agency will work with the State Department to decide if companies are following these rules. If a company that got help is later found not to be following these rules, two things will happen. First, any goods or services the company makes in Latin America or the Caribbean will not get duty free treatment under section four, unless they are made in a free trade zone or covered by a free trade agreement. Second, the government agency will change the interest rate on any loans to the company to the normal market interest rate. For actions related to help under section three, the appropriate government agency means the DFC. For actions related to duty free treatment under section four, it means the United States Trade Representative.

sections.title: Section 6  Expenses Paid for with Tariffs Collected from the People's Republic of China
sections.text: A special trust fund is created in the United States Treasury. Money for this trust fund comes from tariffs (import taxes) collected on goods made in China. Money from this trust fund will be moved to the General Fund of the Treasury to make up for money lost because of the help provided by the DFC under this law. The rules for how and when money is moved into and out of this trust fund will be similar to the rules in section nine thousand six hundred one of the Internal Revenue Code.

sections.title: Section 7  Amendments to the BUILD Act of 2018
sections.text: This section changes the Better Utilization of Investments Leading to Development Act of two thousand eighteen. It adds two new goals for the Act. The ninth goal is to help United States economic growth by giving priority to United States owned businesses when providing support. The tenth goal is to help United States national security by giving priority to producing goods in critical industries, as decided by the Corporation working with the Department of Homeland Security. This section also adds a new rule that prohibits the Corporation from providing support for companies owned or controlled by foreign governments, except in limited cases. The Corporation can provide feasibility studies and technical assistance to companies owned or controlled by foreign governments that are not foreign adversaries. A foreign adversary means a foreign government that has a long term pattern or serious instances of conduct that significantly hurts the national security of the United States or the security and safety of United States citizens.

sections.title: Section 8  Trade Negotiating Authority
sections.text: The United States Trade Representative must start negotiations to get trade agreements with each Latin American or Caribbean country that does not already have a free trade agreement with the United States, if the country meets certain conditions. The conditions are that the country is taking steps to reduce illegal immigration, taking steps to reduce economic dependence on China, and allows Taiwan to establish and maintain a commercial office in the country.

sections.title: Section 9  Agreements for Cooperation Pursuant to Section 123 of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954
sections.text: The President can start negotiations with Latin American and Caribbean countries to get agreements that allow the sale of nuclear reactors to these countries or to qualified companies that get help under this law. This can only happen if the President decides that selling nuclear reactors will not threaten United States national security and if the countries or companies meet the same conditions described in section eight. The Administrator of the United States Agency for International Development can work with the Secretary of Energy and the DFC to provide technical help and expertise about electrical power grids and energy efficiency improvements related to these nuclear reactor sales.

sections.title: Section 10  Temporary Increased Expensing for Relocating Manufacturing from the People's Republic of China to a Latin American or Caribbean Country
sections.text: This section creates special tax benefits for companies that move manufacturing from China to Latin American or Caribbean countries. For qualified relocated manufacturing property that is put into service by a qualified manufacturer after this law starts and before January first, two thousand thirty eight, the property gets special tax treatment. It will be treated as qualified property under tax law, get a seventy five percent applicable percentage for tax deductions, and certain other tax restrictions will not apply. Qualified relocated manufacturing property means qualified property or nonresidential real property that is put into service in a Latin American or Caribbean country by a qualified manufacturer when they move manufacturing operations. A qualified relocation of manufacturing means moving the manufacturing of any physical product from China to a Latin American or Caribbean country. The manufacturing is considered relocated even if the actual equipment used was not moved, as long as the same type of product is being made in the new location and the increase in production in the Latin American or Caribbean country is at least as much as the decrease in production in China. A qualified manufacturer is any person who manufactures physical products, has been approved by the Secretary of the Treasury under the requirements in section five, and has made a binding agreement to meet the requirements of section five that can be enforced like the rules in section five.

sections.title: Section 11  Definitions
sections.text: In this law, DFC means the United States International Development Finance Corporation. Qualified corporation does not include a company owned by a government (called a state owned enterprise). Qualified moving costs means the costs of moving inventory, equipment, and supplies from China to a Latin American or Caribbean country, and the costs of worker training and building new facilities. Latin American or Caribbean country and Western Hemisphere mean a country in the Caribbean Sea, South America, Central America, and Mexico, but do not include Cuba or Venezuela unless certain conditions are met. Cuba or Venezuela can be included if the Secretary of State determines and tells Congress that the government of that country has held free and fair elections with international observers and scheduled future elections, respects human rights, is taking significant steps to privatize its economy and create a free market, allows international help with humanitarian and development assistance, has freed all unlawfully detained United States citizens and political prisoners, has expelled all foreign adversary security services from the country, and the previous authorities have given up their illegitimate claim to power. Federal funds rate means the discount window primary credit interest rate most recently published on the Federal Reserve Statistical Release on selected interest rates, commonly called the H point one five release.
